lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontiers: infrared, oxygen-independent, bioluminescence
We're in the final segment, so let me give you a tour of the frontiers — the directions in which fluorescent-protein technology is actively pushing.
Near-infrared fluorescent proteins.
A major limitation of everything we've discussed is that none of the colors penetrate deep into tissue.
Visible light is heavily scattered and absorbed by tissue components — hemoglobin, melanin, water.
The wavelength window where tissue is most transparent is the near-infrared, roughly 650 to 900 nanometers.
None of the Aequorea or coral fluorescent proteins emit there.

But there's a completely different protein family — the bacterial phytochromes — that bind a small molecule called biliverdin, a breakdown product of heme that's already present in mammalian cells, and that can be engineered into fluorescent proteins emitting in the near-infrared.
The iRFP family, pioneered by Vladislav Verkhusha's lab, are bacterial-phytochrome-derived fluorescent proteins that use biliverdin already abundant in the cell as their chromophore.

They emit beautifully in the near-infrared, and they make it possible to image deep into intact tissues — through entire mice, in some cases — for applications in cancer biology, immunology, and whole-animal imaging.
They've been steadily getting brighter as more engineering work has been done.
Oxygen-independent fluorescent proteins.
Recall from Segment 4 that GFP needs oxygen to mature its chromophore.
That's a problem for imaging strict anaerobes, hypoxic tumor cores, anoxic biofilm interiors.
A small protein called UnaG, from the Japanese eel, gets around this by binding the small molecule bilirubin to make a fluorescent complex — and crucially, this fluorescence doesn't require oxygen.
UnaG and related proteins open up imaging in anaerobic environments where conventional fluorescent proteins are dark.
MiniSOG and photochemistry beyond fluorescence.
Most fluorescent proteins are engineered away from doing extra photochemistry — that's the bleaching to be avoided.
But there's a class of proteins, with miniSOG as the prototype, deliberately designed to generate reactive oxygen species when illuminated.
Why?
Because singlet oxygen has uses.
It can stain cellular structures for electron microscopy.
It can be used to inactivate specific proteins on demand.
It's a small chemical hammer that you can wield with light.
So fluorescent-protein-like tools have moved beyond fluorescence into the broader space of light-driven chemistry inside cells.
NanoLuc and bioluminescence.
A completely different approach: instead of fluorescence — which requires excitation light — use bioluminescence, light produced enzymatically with no external illumination at all.
The classical bioluminescent enzymes are firefly luciferase and Renilla luciferase.
NanoLuc is an engineered small luciferase, only about 19 kilodaltons, that produces strong blue light from a synthetic substrate called furimazine.
Why bother, when fluorescent proteins are so good?
Because bioluminescence has no excitation light — and that means no autofluorescence from the sample, no photobleaching of the reporter, and no phototoxicity to the cell.
For very-long-term imaging, for whole-animal imaging where excitation light can't penetrate, for highly sensitive low-background readouts, bioluminescence is unmatched.
There are now bioluminescent biosensors, and bioluminescence-FRET pairs combining NanoLuc with fluorescent acceptors for very sensitive sensor readouts.
Single-molecule applications.
At the frontier of sensitivity is using fluorescent proteins and dyes to track individual molecules in living cells.
The challenge is photon budget — each molecule emits a finite number of photons before bleaching, and you have to extract every bit of information from those photons.
This is where the brightest dyes, the most photostable proteins, and clever data analysis converge to push the limits of what's measurable.
I want to close with a reflection that ties the whole lecture together.
The arc of fluorescent protein development is, in a way, a parable of how modern biology actually works.
It started with curiosity.
A man, with a bucket, collecting glowing jellyfish for years because he wanted to know how they made light.
It survived a long fallow period when nobody saw the application.
It exploded when someone — Chalfie — saw that the gene could be expressed in another organism.
It was systematically engineered — by Tsien and many others — into a rainbow palette spanning the visible spectrum.
It was extended into sensors that measure things, not just mark them.

It was paired with microscopy that exploits its photophysics, with switchable variants that broke the diffraction limit, with complementary tools like HaloTag and synthetic dyes that fill in its gaps.
And it continues — with infrared variants for deep tissue, with bioluminescent and oxygen-independent alternatives, with ever-more-clever engineered variants for ever-more-demanding applications.

The whole field is a multi-generational collaboration, built on a curious natural observation by patient stepwise engineering, with no single moment of genius but a continuous accumulation of improvements driven by both the demands of biological questions and the creativity of protein chemists.
That is how most of modern biology actually works.
The fluorescent protein story is just an unusually clear and beautiful example of it.
If you remember nothing else, remember three things.
One: the beta barrel with the autocatalytic chromophore is the central molecular invention — a protein soda can wrapped around three amino acids that cook themselves into a fluorescent group.
That single design explains essentially every property of every GFP-family protein you'll ever encounter.
Two: the rubric — brightness, photostability, maturation, monomericity, pH sensitivity, folding — is how you choose among the dozens of variants.
Run it every time.

Three: fluorescent proteins are a tool, embedded in a broader optical toolkit that includes self-labeling tags, synthetic dyes, bioluminescent reporters, optogenetic actuators, and the microscopy modalities to image all of it.
The best modern experiments combine these deliberately, matching each tool to its question.
The molecular glow stick changed cell biology.
The next decade will see it change again — brighter, smaller, deeper, more sensitive, more controllable.
The frontier is open.
Some of you will be the ones who push it.
Thanks, everyone — good work today.
